The key word is past tense verbs. Ma'adi. Al-fa'al ma'adi. That when you want to negate a past tense verb and such and such person did not do blank, then you use ma. Right? You don't use la. You get it? So, if you want to say, I didn't do that, you don't say, la fa'altu. You say, ma fa'altu. Right? For example, ma fa'aluhu illa qaleelun minhum. Right? It doesn't say, la fa'aluhu. Right? Give you more examples from the Quran of a past tense verb being used with ma.

Right? And we learned that this is actually a negative question, right? You're saying, is it not like that? Right? So it's kind of like a trick question. Is it not like that? So, if you answer نعم, like normally in Arabic, you'd be saying نعم. If you want to answer in the affirmative, you want to say yes, it is like that. Normally, you would say نعم, because that's the answer to a question normally. However, if you're saying أليس

18
00:17:00,480 --> 00:18:00,059
[SPEAKER_00] كذلك, and then you say نعم, then you were actually saying the opposite. It's a negative, right? Because the question contains ليس. So you're saying, is it not like that? So then, if you were to answer نعم, that means you're saying, yes, it is not like that. Do you get it? So, that's why when you say أليس كذلك, and you want to reply in the affirmative that it is like that, then you would say بلا. Because بلا comes with, within it, it comes in the meaning of a negative. So basically, as we know, a double negative makes a positive. Right? So, is it not like that? And if it says, basically you're saying no, meaning, yes, it is like that. Do you guys understand? So, بلا takes that single negative and makes it a double negative, which makes it a positive, which means yes. Get it? So, they're just trying to show that difference. So, it's the same thing over here.

Then Allah says بلا. Why? Because the question was, does he think that we will not? So you yourself have to figure out based on the context, the sentence, that this can't be ذَهَبْتَ It can't be ذَهَبْتِ because you're not talking to Layla, you're talking about Layla. It can't be ذَهَبْتُ because you're not talking about yourself. Therefore, it has to be ذَهَبَتْ because it's talking about Layla, who is a female, singular. Got it? So that's the process that your mind has to go through. Again, at some point it becomes autopilot where as soon as you see the sentence, it becomes clear what the meaning is and you don't have to think about it.

This is called لا النافية للجنس لا Right. So normally لا won't do anything to the word after it. However when you want to completely negate something Right. title: Advanced Practice with Past Tense Verbs and Negation
summary: The session extended practice with past tense verbs (فعل ماضي), focusing on conjugations for different pronouns and correct usage of negation with 'ma' (ما). It also introduced the sisters of 'inna (إنّ) including 'la'kan (لكن)', 'kathththina (كَأَنَّ)', and 'li'aanna (لِأَنَّ)' in various contexts. The class included examples from Quranic Arabic and emphasized proper verb-subject agreement.
key_points: Past tense verbs (فعل ماضي) conjugations: e.g., ذهب – dhahaba (he went), ذهبت – dhahabta (you [fem.] went).
Negation in past tense using 'ma' (ما)，e.g., ما فعلت – ma fa‘altu (I did not do it).
Sisters of 'inna (إنّ): introduction to لَكِنَّ (but), كَأَنَّ (as if), and لِأَنَّ (because) with examples.
Verb-subject agreement rules, especially when verbs precede the subject (الفاعل), e.g., كَسَرَ الکتاب (kassara al-kitāb) vs. كَسَرتَ الکتاب (kassart(a) al-kitāb).
Importance of vowel changes in derived verbs, such as سَمِعَ (sami‘a) and فَهِمَ (fahima), maintaining fatha on initial and final letters.
questions: [00:15:00] Q: How do you negate past tense verbs? — A: Use 'ma' with the verb, e.g., مَا ذَهَبْتُ (ma dhahabtu).
[00:29:00] Q: Why is 'la' used instead of 'ma' in negation for certain sentences? — A: 'La' negates future or present, while 'ma' specifically negates past verbs.
